_source: Hand-curated from published Wav2Vec2-Phoneme behaviour and well-documented L2 substitution patterns. Confusion targets are drawn from linguamatch's L1_PHONEMES table (the phonemes linguamatch most cares about classifying correctly) plus voiceprint's PROBE_SETS stretch phonemes (Mandarin retroflexes/affricates, Arabic pharyngeals/emphatics/uvulars).
_caveats: Weights are conservative starting estimates from descriptive linguistics literature and the canonical L2-acquisition substitution sets. They have NOT been validated against a labelled audio corpus.

References cited generically as 'common L2 substitution' or 'visually similar place of articulation' are well-documented patterns in phonetics textbooks (e.g. Ladefoged & Maddieson 1996, 'The Sounds of the World's Languages'); per spec §6.1 that's acceptable provenance.
Targets and near-miss symbols are written in canonical IPA. The smoothing service normalises Wav2Vec2's eSpeak-style vocabulary back to IPA via phoneme_alphabet.json before applying these weights.

Future work (Loop 4+ or a Loop 3 hotfix): validate weights against labelled audio. The most likely calibration adjustment is downweighting place-of-articulation confusions (Wav2Vec2 generally distinguishes manner better than place) and upweighting manner confusions on affricates.

near_misses.source: Common Wav2Vec2 confusion; dental->alveolar fricative is the canonical L2 English substitution for non-thorn-bearing L1s (French, German, Russian, Mandarin, Japanese, etc.).
Common Wav2Vec2 confusion; same voiceless-fricative manner with shifted place (dental->labiodental). Documented substitution in many L2 inventories.
Stopping of /th/ documented in some Indian English varieties.
Common Wav2Vec2 confusion; dental->alveolar voiced. Most common L2 substitution alongside /th/->/s/.
Common Wav2Vec2 confusion; voiced labiodental as a near-equivalent. Typical for L1s without dental fricatives.
Voiced sibilant substitution; documented in some L1s.
Rhotic->approximant confusion. Common in Japanese L1; Wav2Vec2 often confuses /ɹ/ and /w/ when the speaker doesn't fully retroflex.
Tap substitution for English approximant /ɹ/. Spanish/Italian/Polish L1s often produce a tap when reading English r.
Liquid confusion; documented in some East Asian L1s (e.g. Japanese r/l merger).
Voiced labiodental->voiced bilabial. Spanish and several other L1s lack /v/ and substitute /b/ (or the bilabial approximant).
Approximant substitution for /v/. Common in some Indian English varieties.
Voicing-place confusion. Common in German, Russian, and Polish L1s where /v/ is the labiodental phoneme used in place of English /w/.
Velar->alveolar nasal confusion. Common in word-final position for many L1s; Wav2Vec2 sometimes drops the velar cue.
Affricate->fricative confusion. Wav2Vec2 frequently misses the closure burst, especially in fast speech.
Closure-only realisation. The model picks up the stop and misses the fricative release.
Postalveolar->retroflex affricate confusion; documented in Mandarin L1 speakers reading English.
Affricate->fricative confusion. Mirror image of /tʃ/->/ʃ/.
Closure-only realisation; mirror of /tʃ/->/t/.
Affricate->approximant in some L1s (e.g. Spanish reading English orthographic 'j').
Alveolo-palatal->postalveolar affricate. Wav2Vec2 routinely conflates these two on Mandarin and Polish audio.
Affricate->fricative within the alveolo-palatal/postalveolar family.
Voiced mirror of /tɕ/->/tʃ/ confusion. Mandarin and Polish L1s.
Affricate->fricative voiced.
Aspirated alveolo-palatal often emitted as plain postalveolar by the model. Mandarin pinyin q is the canonical example.
Aspiration mark dropped; Wav2Vec2 can miss the aspiration cue at low SNR.
Affricate place confusion (alveolo-palatal->alveolar) with aspiration preserved.
Affricate->fricative; closure burst missed. Documented for German, Russian, Mandarin L1s.
Closure-only realisation; mirror of /tʃ/->/t/.
Aspiration dropped; common across all aspirated obstruents under low-energy conditions.
Affricate->fricative + aspiration; combined manner+release confusion.
Retroflex->postalveolar; the most common L2 English substitution for Mandarin zh.
Affricate->fricative within the retroflex series.
Retroflex affricate->alveolar affricate when retroflex cue is missed.
Voiced mirror of /ʈʂ/->/tʃ/; Mandarin zh-voiced equivalent.
Affricate->fricative voiced retroflex.
Retroflex->postalveolar fricative; Mandarin sh -> English sh.
Retroflex->alveolar when retroflex cue is missed.
Voiced mirror of /ʂ/->/ʃ/.
Retroflex->alveolar voiced.
Retroflex approximant->postalveolar approximant; Mandarin r often produced like English r.
Liquid confusion in some L1s.
Retroflex->alveolar stop. Hindi/Urdu/Pashto retroflex T heard as English t by Wav2Vec2.
Retroflex->aspirated alveolar; some Indic-language speakers aspirate the retroflex stop.
Voiced mirror of /ʈ/->/t/; Hindi retroflex D heard as English d.
Pharyngeal->glottal confusion. L2 speakers reading Arabic ayn often produce a glottal stop; Wav2Vec2 follows the same pattern.
Voiceless approximation when pharyngeal voicing is lost.
Pharyngeal->velar voiced fricative confusion; documented in some Arabic dialects.
Pharyngeal->glottal fricative. Most common L2 substitution for Arabic ḥa.
Pharyngeal->velar fricative; Wav2Vec2 confuses pharyngeal and velar fricatives routinely.
Uvular->velar confusion; the canonical L2 substitution for Arabic/Hebrew/Persian qaf.
Some Arabic dialects (Egyptian, Levantine urban) realise /q/ as glottal stop.
Emphatic->plain coronal stop; the canonical L2 substitution for Arabic emphatic T. Voiceprint vocab has no emphatic-t entry, so direct counts are always zero — smoothed evidence is the only signal.
Emphatic->dental plain stop; a finer-grained alternative substitution.
Emphatic->plain alveolar fricative. Same emphasis-dropping pattern as /tˤ/->/t/.
Emphatic->plain voiced alveolar stop. Mirror of /tˤ/->/t/.
Emphatic->plain voiced dental fricative.
Some dialects merge emphatic /ðˤ/ with /z/.
Velar fricative->velar stop. German/Spanish/Russian L1 reading Arabic kh sometimes hits the stop instead of the continuant.
Velar->glottal fricative; Wav2Vec2 confuses these on weak velar friction.
Uvular->velar fricative; the two are often phonetically very close and Wav2Vec2 conflates them.
Uvular->pharyngeal voiceless fricative; minimal-pair distinction often lost.
Voiced velar fricative->voiced velar stop. Common when fricative voicing weakens to closure.
Velar->uvular voiced fricative; complementary to /χ/->/x/.
Uvular->velar voiced fricative; phonetic neighbours often confused.
Uvular->alveolar trill; the French/German r vs Spanish/Italian r dichotomy is sometimes flattened by Wav2Vec2.
Trill->tap; Spanish-style alternation. Wav2Vec2 picks the tap when the trill has fewer than 2 closures.
Trill->English approximant; L1 English speakers reading languages with trills.
Tap->trill; a vigorous tap may be misclassified as a short trill.
Tap->alveolar stop; documented in some Spanish L1 speakers reading English.
Lax->tense high front vowel. L1s without the tense/lax distinction (Spanish, Italian, etc.) merge /ɪ/ with /i/.
Lax->tense high back vowel. Same merger pattern as /ɪ/->/i/.
Schwa->open-mid back; for L1s without true schwa, Wav2Vec2 sometimes assigns the open-mid back vowel.
Near-open->open-mid front vowel. Common L2 substitution for English /æ/.
Near-open->open front; some L1s collapse /æ/ into the cardinal /a/.
Open-mid->close-mid; minimal-pair confusion in L1s with only one front mid vowel.
